notes: A 2016 revision note in the XML records structural review, removal of prose fragments, and changes to multiple element structures.
Repeated PB elements across parallel streams are print-page milestones, not distinct manuscript leaves.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Frederick Simmons, ed., The Lay Folks Mass Book; or, The Manner of Hearing Mass, EETS Original Series 71 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1879).
bibliographic.publisher: Published for the Early English Text Society by N. Trübner & Co.
bibliographic.title: The Lay Folks Mass Book; or, The Manner of Hearing Mass, with Rubrics and Devotions for the People, in Four Texts, and Offices in English according to the Use of York

entities.description: The immediate source edition for U-M. The encoded subset spans the LFMB parallel-text section, four bidding prayers, the York Hours, Appendices III-V, and selected notes and fragments.
entities.label: Simmons's 1879 Lay Folks Mass Book
notes: CME00052 includes edition pages 2-60, 64-80, 82-87, 122-154, and 367-371, plus the unnumbered four-text list and York Hours part title; these correspond to PDF pages 79-138, 142-165, 200-232, and 445-449 in the cached Internet Archive scan, with gaps.
On pp. 367-371 Simmons compares the printed fragments with the Vertewis of the Mess and explicitly cites Lumby's Ratis Raving edition at p. 113, line 3943.
Simmons's title-page phrase 'from manuscripts of the Xth to the XVth century' describes the volume's broad source range, not the date range of LFMB itself.

rights.basis: The underlying texts and medieval manuscript witnesses substantially predate modern copyright terms.
rights.component: Underlying medieval textual content

next_steps: Ask York Minster Library to reconcile Simmons's and Henderson's description with its current MSS XVI catalog and provide the prayer folios.
If images are supplied, request the whole codex and map the prayers within it.
openQuestions.question: What is the current shelfmark and exact folio range of the Fothergill York Manual used for Bidding Prayers III-IV?

next_steps: Send shelfmark-specific reproduction enquiries to the British Library, Corpus Christi College, CUL, Caius, Newnham, Liverpool, York Minster, and Bodleian.
Request whole manuscripts where feasible and map each relevant folio span inside the delivered object.
openQuestions.question: Do complete public or requestable digital facsimiles exist for LFMB witnesses B-I and the uncached appendix and note witnesses?

next_steps: Treat Simmons's Rouen/French-source hypothesis as historical conjecture unless a current specialist source supplies new evidence.
Keep the lost or hypothetical source separate from the nine extant English versions.
notes: Smith finds no evidence that the English LFMB was composed much before the late-fourteenth-century B witness.
openQuestions.question: Can any pre-archetypal source behind the English LFMB versions, including Simmons's proposed French work by 'dam Jeremy,' be securely identified?

Henderson's Manuale et processionale ad usum Eboracensis

reviewNotes: The Lay Folks' Mass Book is Simmons's Victorian title. Modern scholarship treats the nine surviving medieval copies as distinct versions rather than transparent witnesses to one recoverable wording.

Simmons's four main LFMB text streams derive from witnesses B, C, E, and F. A and D supply selected readings only. G, H, and I were identified after 1879 and are collateral witnesses, not ancestors of CME00052.

Appendix IV is mapped inside the complete Vernon codex at fols. 302v-303v, IIIF canvases 491-493. The NLS A fragment is mapped at fols. 57r-58v, pages/canvases 129-132. Bidding Prayer II is mapped inside MS Widener 1 at fols. 105r-107r, IIIF sequences 110-112.

The whole-object rule is applied to manuscript facsimiles whenever the provider exposes the whole object. Completeness statements distinguish provider-object coverage from physical-object surface coverage.
Medieval text, nineteenth-century edition, modern encoding, scholarly article, repository artifact, and manuscript-image rights are recorded as separate layers.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a selective encoding of Thomas Frederick Simmons's 1879 EETS volume, not an encoding of one medieval codex or one homogeneous medieval work. U-M retained the four parallel Lay Folks' Mass Book texts B, C, E, and F with readings from A and D; four York bidding prayers; the York Hours of the Cross; Appendices III-V; and selected notes and fragments. It omitted large portions of Simmons's preface, introduction, appendices, notes, glossary, and index. Simmons worked through named nineteenth-century transcripts for the principal LFMB witnesses, rearranged the disordered E text against B, expanded manuscript abbreviations in italics, marked supplied text and damage typographically, and represented rubrication in Clarendon type. His encoded notes also quote Lumby's 1870 edition of the Vertewis of the Mess from Cambridge University Library MS Kk.1.5. The encoded selection maps to discontiguous printed pages 2-60, 64-80, 82-87, 122-154, and 367-371, plus two unnumbered part pages. Complete provider objects have been cached for the 1879 edition, Lumby's 1870 supporting edition, NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1, the Vernon Manuscript, and Harvard MS Widener 1; each local copy maps the relevant portion inside the full object. The Harvard object is a complete 182-image microfilm/provider inventory but is not a physically complete surrogate because exterior boards, spine, and edge views are not exposed.

catalogSummary.title: The Lay Folks Mass Book and offices according to the Use of York (Simmons selection)

catalogingSubject.label: The Lay Folks Mass Book and offices according to the Use of York (Simmons selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The exact edition-level selection represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00052: Simmons's four parallel Lay Folks' Mass Book texts B, C, E, and F with selected readings from A and D; Bidding Prayers II-V; the York Hours of the Cross; Appendices III-V; and selected notes and fragments. The record does not collapse these independent medieval works and versions, their manuscript witnesses, Simmons's full 1879 volume, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML into one abstract medieval work.

contentParts.label: The Lay Folks' Mass Book in four texts
notes: A and D are variant-reading sources, not fifth and sixth parallel streams. G-I are later-discovered collateral versions and are absent from Simmons.
contentParts.summary: Four parallel and materially distinct LFMB versions B, C, E, and F, with selected A and D readings and a detached F prayer. The verse instructs an audience how to attend, pray, and behave through the Mass.

contentParts.label: Bidding Prayer II
contentParts.summary: A Middle English and Latin York communal intercession for the Church, realm, parish, living, sick, travelers, women in childbirth, debtors, prisoners, benefactors, and dead.

contentParts.label: Bidding Prayer III
contentParts.summary: A longer Middle English and Latin bidding prayer from the York Minster Fothergill Manual.

contentParts.label: Bidding Prayer IV
contentParts.summary: A shorter later addition in the York Minster Manual, praying for the Church, kingdom, parish, travelers, sick, and dead.

contentParts.label: Bidding Prayer V
contentParts.summary: Preces pro diebus Dominicis from Wynkyn de Worde's 1509 York Manual, combining Middle English petitions and Latin psalms, suffrages, antiphons, and collects.

contentParts.label: York Hours of the Cross

locators.value: English fols. 1r-2v; Latin in discontiguous passages through fol. 24v

notes: The English Compline anthem comes from a Cambridge manuscript; the Recommendation comes from the 1543 Prymer; some Latin titles and bracketed words come from printed York Hours.
contentParts.summary: A bilingual cycle from Matins through Compline meditating on Christ's Passion at the canonical hours. Simmons juxtaposes English verse and Latin text and supplies missing portions from other manuscript and printed sources.

contentParts.label: Appendix III: Preparation before receiving the Sacrament
notes: Ashmole 1286 is the source of this appendix, not an LFMB witness.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English prose devotional instruction on six considerations before receiving the Eucharist.

contentParts.label: Appendix IV: A Treatise of the Manner and Mede of the Mass
contentParts.summary: A 688-line Middle English devotional poem explaining the benefits of hearing Mass and the actions and prayers appropriate during it.

contentParts.label: Appendix V: Merita Missae
contentParts.summary: A Middle English verse guide to daily devotion and hearing Mass, attributed by Simmons to John Lydgate.

contentParts.label: Selected notes and comparative fragments

locators.value: Vertewis of the Mess, pp. 113-115, lines 3917-3993; cached PDF pp. 131-133

notes: This is not the complete notes section and should not be cataloged as one medieval work.
contentParts.summary: A discontiguous extract from Simmons's notes, including the opening of Lydgate's Virtutes Missarum, comparisons with Lumby's edition of the Vertewis of the Mess, other manuscript comparisons, Latin quotations, and a final stanza from Harley MS 2251.

dateStatements.display: The source edition was published in 1879
notes: This dates the edited selection, not the medieval components.

dateStatements.display: LFMB is first known from the late-fourteenth-century Royal MS 17 B XVII; modern scholarship finds no evidence for English composition much earlier
notes: This is first-known-attestation evidence, not a firm composition range and not support for Simmons's speculative early French-source theory.

dateStatements.display: The encoded medieval components are chiefly witnessed in manuscripts of the late fourteenth and fifteenth centuries; Bidding Prayer V is taken from a 1509 print
notes: The range must not be interpreted as one work's composition period.

scope.description: Broad attestation range across independent components

extent.description: Ten cataloged content groups are represented by thirteen BODY-level DIV1 elements: a four-text list, the main LFMB parallel section, a detached comparison prayer from F, four bidding-prayer divisions, a York Hours part title and text, three appendices, and selected notes/fragments. The BODY has 304 P, 104 LG, 3,172 L, 765 NOTE, 181 PB, and 994 MILESTONE elements. These are encoding metrics, not canonical medieval-work counts; PBs repeat across parallel versions.

formStatements.description: The selection deliberately combines verse, prose, and mixed liturgical sequences; editorial prose is also retained.

scope.description: Principal verse works
Vernacular prayer and devotional prose with embedded Latin
Primary language of the medieval vernacular texts and prayers

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Latin is structurally substantial in the bidding prayers and York Hours, not merely an isolated quotation language.

scope.description: Liturgical incipits, antiphons, suffrages, prayers, quotations, and the parallel Latin Hours
Simmons's headings, glosses, marginal summaries, prose translation, and notes

notes: The preferred title is intentionally supplied and selection-qualified; the U-M and Simmons title forms remain separately searchable.
LFMB witness dates, dialects, and regional circulation are not silently converted into one composition date or place.
The G, H, and I LFMB versions are useful comparison sources but are not ancestors of Simmons 1879 or CME00052.
Manuscript-image completeness and rights are maintained in the paired lineage manifest, not inferred from work metadata.

next_steps: Consult component-specific critical editions and repertories rather than projecting witness dates onto composition.
Record competing attributions part by part, especially for Lydgate material and the York Hours supplements.
openQuestions.question: What dates, places of composition, and responsibility statements can current specialist scholarship defend for each independent bidding prayer, Hours text, appendix, and note fragment?

next_steps: Request catalog reconciliation and a whole-codex reproduction from York Minster Library.
Map both prayers inside the delivered full object before upgrading the record.

subjects.label: Intercession for the living and the dead

scope.description: Four parallel versions plus variant readings and later collateral witnesses

summaries.value: A selective electronic edition of medieval English materials for hearing and understanding Mass. Its core is four distinct manuscript versions of the verse Lay Folks' Mass Book, presented with variant readings. It also includes York bidding prayers for communal intercession, a bilingual York Hours of the Cross, prose preparation for Communion, two verse guides to hearing Mass, Lydgate-attributed material, and comparative fragments. Simmons's modern arrangement, glosses, translations, and apparatus remain integral to the object represented here.

CME00052 omits substantial runs of Simmons's volume, including most front matter, Appendix I, Appendix II, most notes, the glossary, and the index. Its medieval contents are neither one authorial work nor the contents of one manuscript.

notes: The qualifier signals that CME00052 is an edition-level selection and avoids implying that every component is part of the medieval LFMB poem.
titles.value: The Lay Folks Mass Book and offices according to the Use of York (Simmons selection)

notes: The broad manuscript-century phrase belongs to the edition title and is not a date statement for the LFMB poem.

Its core is four distinct manuscript versions of the verse Lay Folks' Mass Book, presented with variant readings. It also includes York bidding prayers for communal intercession, a bilingual York Hours of the Cross, prose preparation for Communion, two verse guides to hearing Mass, Lydgate-attributed material, and comparative fragments.
